mappings.rationale: Dunlop's VSI directly covers weather phenomena including clouds, precipitation, storms, and weather forecasting—the primary subject matter of this section.
Maslin covers climate types, classification systems, climatic variation, and the factors that generate climate—core topics of this section's treatment of climate.
Directly addresses climatic change, greenhouse warming, human effects on climate, and the evidence for historical climate shifts discussed in this section.
Palmer covers atmospheric moisture, condensation processes, and wind systems that drive weather patterns and storms described in this section.
McGuire covers severe weather hazards including hurricanes, typhoons, tornadoes, and flooding, directly relevant to the section's treatment of storms and extreme weather.
Lenton discusses climate as part of the integrated Earth system, including solar radiation, the greenhouse effect, and ocean-atmosphere interactions that drive climate.
Redfern provides context for understanding weather and climate within the broader Earth system, including atmospheric processes and climate variability.
Stow covers ocean-atmosphere interactions including El Nino/Southern Oscillation and the oceanic influences on climate discussed in this section.
Judge covers solar radiation variability and the solar constant, directly relevant to the section's discussion of solar radiation as a factor generating climate.
Evans covers glacial climate periods and ice-albedo feedback, relevant to the section's discussion of climatic change, ice ages, and the Earth's albedo.
Woodward covers Pleistocene climate oscillations and glacial-interglacial cycles, directly relevant to climatic change and the evidence from landscape features associated with glaciations.
Middleton covers arid climate types and their distribution, relevant to the section's treatment of climatic types and the Koppen classification system.
Covers Arctic climate, polar weather systems, and the effects of climate change on polar regions, relevant to climatic variation and change.
Dodds covers Antarctic climate and weather patterns, relevant to polar climate types and climate change evidence from ice cores.
Dobson addresses the political dimensions of climate change policy, relevant to the section's discussion of human effects on global climate through fossil fuel burning.
Ellis discusses human-driven climate change including greenhouse warming from carbon dioxide buildup, directly relevant to the section's treatment of human effects on climate.
Ali addresses sustainable responses to climate change, relevant to the section's coverage of greenhouse warming and human activities affecting global climate.
Smith covers chaos theory and its applications to weather prediction, directly relevant to the section's discussion of limits to predictability in long-range forecasting.
Jelley discusses solar radiation, wind energy, and the transition from fossil fuels, relevant to the section's coverage of solar radiation factors and human effects on climate.
